project.name: Sublemonable
project.description: The complete security onion. Every layer assumes the one beneath it has already failed. v1.5 adds plausible deniability, dead drop mode, decoy traffic, multi-hop relay, and Tor-first architecture — all before the blockchain layer of v2.
project.philosophy: Security is not a feature. It is an onion. Layer 4 failing must not expose layer 5. Layer 2 failing must not expose layer 4. Every layer is designed as if all others have already been compromised.

security_onion.description: Complete layered security model as of v1.5. Each layer is independent.

layer_1_physical.name: Physical Access
features: Panic wipe — 3-finger long press, full wipe in under 2 seconds
Duress PIN — alternate PIN that silently wipes while appearing to unlock
Plausible deniability — two vaults, one app, no evidence second vault exists
FLAG_SECURE — OS-level screenshot block on Android
Biometric lock — Face ID / Touch ID / fingerprint
Background app blur — content hidden when app not in foreground

layer_2_network.name: Network
features: TLS 1.3 — all transport encrypted
Certificate pinning — MITM impossible
Tor-first — default connection via Tor, clearnet is fallback not default
Multi-hop relay — 3-hop onion routing through relay network
Decoy traffic — continuous cover traffic, active and idle indistinguishable
obfs4 transport — Tor traffic obfuscated, looks like generic HTTPS

layer_3_identity.name: Identity
features: No phone number required
No email required
UUID routing — never exposed to other parties
Sealed Sender — relay network never knows sender identity
Dead drop mode — no direct communication channel between parties
QR-only contact exchange — no central discovery

layer_4_message.name: Message
features: Signal Protocol — E2EE with forward secrecy per message
Double Ratchet — new key every message
256-byte padding — message size analysis impossible
Burn on read — destruction on first open
TTL destruction — server purges on schedule regardless
Zero server logs — nothing stored to subpoena

layer_5_storage.name: Storage
features: Argon2id vault derivation — identical timing for all passphrases
Plausible deniability vaults — AES-256-GCM, cryptographically separate
AES-256-GCM at rest — all local data encrypted
Secure Enclave / Android Keystore — hardware-backed key operations
Memory zeroing — key material cleared from RAM immediately after use
Secure delete — overwrite before free on all sensitive data

connection_modes.description: Three user-selectable connection modes. Selectable in Settings → Network → Connection Mode.

standard.name: Standard
standard.description: Tor routing + single relay hop + Sealed Sender. Suitable for everyday use.
standard.indicator: Single lemon slice segment lit in settings

stealth.name: Stealth
stealth.description: Tor routing + 3-hop onion relay + decoy traffic. For sensitive communications.
stealth.indicator: Three lemon slice segments lit in settings
stealth.latency_warning: Messages may take 2-4 seconds longer to deliver

ghost.name: Ghost
ghost.description: Tor + 3-hop relay + continuous decoy traffic + dead drop only. No persistent identity visible on the network.
ghost.indicator: All lemon slice segments lit, pulsing slowly
ghost.latency_warning: Dead drop pickup requires recipient to be online or check manually
ghost.note: In Ghost mode, all messages are dead drops. No direct channel exists.

plausible_deniability.description: Two completely separate encrypted vaults behind two different passphrases. No cryptographic evidence the second vault exists.
plausible_deniability.principle: Both vaults are real registered accounts that can send and receive real messages. The decoy is not fake. It is a real identity.

vault_architecture.vault_count: Never stored anywhere. App tries passphrase against key slots. If it decrypts, that is the active profile.
vault_architecture.key_derivation: Argon2id with identical parameters for all passphrases — memory: 65536 KB, iterations: 3, parallelism: 4
vault_architecture.timing_attack_prevention: Both passphrases take identical wall-clock time to derive. No shortcut on decoy path. A stopwatch cannot distinguish them.
vault_architecture.storage_isolation: Vault A and Vault B use completely separate encrypted storage namespaces. No shared tables, no shared files.
vault_architecture.storage_padding: Decoy vault storage padded with random encrypted blocks proportional to total app storage — prevents size anomaly detection
vault_architecture.key_slots: App maintains 2 key slots (expandable to 4 in future). Each slot is a 32-byte random salt + AES-256-GCM encrypted vault key. Trying a passphrase attempts decryption of all slots. First successful decryption wins.
vault_architecture.no_vault_index: The integer number of vaults is never stored. Slot decryption failure is indistinguishable from wrong passphrase.
vault_architecture.server_accounts: Each vault has its own independent server account, identity key, and prekey bundle. The server cannot link them.
vault_architecture.notifications: Push notifications delivered as encrypted signals only. When decoy vault is active, real vault notifications are silenced and queued encrypted. No notification reveals real vault existence.

forensic_protections.file_timestamps: All vault files use normalized timestamps. OS-level file metadata does not reveal activity patterns.
forensic_protections.sqlite_wal: WAL journal files encrypted and wiped immediately on checkpoint. No recoverable fragments.
forensic_protections.indexeddb_web: All IndexedDB entries are encrypted blobs keyed by derived identifiers. Schema reveals nothing about vault count or content.
forensic_protections.secure_delete: All deleted content overwritten with random bytes before deallocation on all platforms.
forensic_protections.memory_forensics: Decrypted vault contents never written to disk. In-memory only. Zeroed immediately on app background.

decoy_best_practices.activity_reminder: App gently prompts if decoy profile unused for 7+ days: 'Your other profile has been quiet.' No explanation of why.
decoy_best_practices.realistic_contacts: Onboarding suggests adding 2-3 real public-facing contacts to decoy profile
decoy_best_practices.ui_identical: Decoy and real vault are visually identical. Same features, same performance, same everything.
decoy_best_practices.naming: Never called hidden vault, secret profile, or deniability in UI. Setup flow says only: 'Add a second passphrase for an alternate profile.'

platform_implementation.web: Custom encrypted storage layer over IndexedDB. All keys derived from vault key. No plaintext schema.
platform_implementation.ios: Separate Keychain entries per vault, keyed by derived fingerprint not vault index. Separate SQLite databases, both encrypted via SQLCipher.
platform_implementation.android: Separate EncryptedSharedPreferences namespaces. Separate encrypted SQLite via SQLCipher. Keystore entries keyed by derived alias.

plausible_deniability.legal_note: User provides passphrase A under compulsion. Decoy profile opens. No cryptographic evidence passphrase B exists. User has not provided false information. This mirrors the VeraCrypt hidden volume legal model.

dead_drop_mode.description: Asynchronous anonymous message deposit. No direct communication channel between sender and recipient. No metadata linking the two parties.
dead_drop_mode.tradecraft_origin: Spy tradecraft technique. Sender leaves message at agreed location. Recipient retrieves it independently. The two never communicate directly.

how_it_works.step_1: Sender creates a dead drop — generates a one-time random drop ID and access token
how_it_works.step_2: Sender encrypts message with recipient's public key (X3DH as normal)
how_it_works.step_3: Sender deposits encrypted envelope at a relay node under the drop ID
how_it_works.step_4: Drop ID and access token shared out-of-band — QR code, separate channel, in-person
how_it_works.step_5: Recipient redeems token at any relay node — no account required for pickup
how_it_works.step_6: Relay verifies token, delivers envelope, immediately destroys drop
how_it_works.step_7: Token is single-use. Replay attack returns empty.
how_it_works.step_8: No record of who deposited or who retrieved exists on any relay

anonymity_properties.sender_identity: Relay never knows. Drop deposited via Tor with no auth.
anonymity_properties.recipient_identity: Relay never knows. Token redemption requires no account.
anonymity_properties.communication_link: No metadata linking sender to recipient exists anywhere.
anonymity_properties.timing: Sender deposits and recipient retrieves at different times. No timing correlation.
anonymity_properties.token_format: 256-bit random token. Single use. HMAC-verified by relay.
anonymity_properties.after_ttl: Drop destroyed whether collected or not. Sender not notified — by design.

ui_flow.create_drop: Compose message → long press send → 'Send as dead drop' → generates QR code containing drop token → share QR out-of-band
ui_flow.redeem_drop: Main screen → '+' → 'Redeem dead drop' → scan QR → message delivered and drop destroyed
ui_flow.ghost_mode: In Ghost connection mode, all messages are automatically dead drops. No direct channels.

server_changes.new_endpoint: POST /api/v1/drops — deposit encrypted envelope + token hash (no auth required, proof-of-work instead)
server_changes.redeem_endpoint: POST /api/v1/drops/redeem — present token, receive envelope, drop destroyed
server_changes.storage: Drop table: id (hash of token), ciphertext (blob), expires_at. Nothing else.
server_changes.no_sender_field: Drop table has no sender column. By design. Server cannot know.
server_changes.proof_of_work: Hashcash puzzle required to deposit — prevents spam without requiring account

decoy_traffic.description: Continuous cover traffic that makes real message sending indistinguishable from idle state. Eliminates traffic analysis.
decoy_traffic.problem_solved: Even with Sealed Sender and Tor, an observer watching network traffic can see: this device sent something at 2:14am. Decoy traffic makes that observation meaningless.

implementation.mechanism: Background service generates fake encrypted envelopes at randomized intervals and submits them to relay nodes
implementation.indistinguishable: Decoy envelopes are padded to identical 256-byte block sizes as real messages. Same Tor circuit. Same relay nodes. Same submission path.
implementation.fake_recipients: Decoy traffic addressed to randomly generated UUIDs that resolve to nowhere. Relay stores briefly, TTL expires, purged. No real delivery.
implementation.timing_randomization: Poisson process interval distribution — not uniform, not predictable. Matches human messaging patterns.

intensity_levels.off: No decoy traffic. Default in Standard mode.
intensity_levels.low: 1 decoy per real message sent. Minimal battery impact.
intensity_levels.medium: Continuous at ~1 per 30-120 seconds randomized. Stealth mode default.
intensity_levels.high: Continuous at ~1 per 5-30 seconds randomized. Ghost mode default.

implementation.user_control: Settings → Privacy → Cover Traffic → Off / Low / Medium / High. Current mode shown as lemon slice fill level.

platform_notes.ios: BGProcessingTask for background decoy generation when app is backgrounded. Degrades gracefully if iOS kills background task.
platform_notes.android: WorkManager periodic task. Foreground service option for maximum reliability.
platform_notes.web: Service Worker background sync. Decoy traffic continues while browser tab is backgrounded.

multi_hop_relay.description: Onion-routed message delivery through a chain of 3 relay nodes. No single node knows both sender and recipient.
multi_hop_relay.inspired_by: Tor's onion routing — adapted for the Sublemonable relay network specifically.

how_it_works.path_selection: Client queries relay registry and selects 3 relay nodes randomly. Nodes in different geographic regions preferred.

onion_encryption.step_1: Client encrypts message for Relay C (innermost layer)
onion_encryption.step_2: Client wraps in encryption for Relay B
onion_encryption.step_3: Client wraps in encryption for Relay A (outermost layer)

how_it_works.relay_a: Knows sender IP (via Tor, so not real IP). Decrypts outer layer. Sees only: forward to Relay B. Does not know final recipient.
how_it_works.relay_b: Knows Relay A. Decrypts middle layer. Sees only: forward to Relay C. Knows neither sender nor recipient.
how_it_works.relay_c: Knows Relay B. Decrypts inner layer. Sees only: recipient ID + encrypted envelope. Does not know sender.
how_it_works.recipient: Picks up from Relay C. Decrypts with Signal Protocol. Sealed Sender reveals true sender only to recipient.

relay_a: Previous hop (Tor exit node — not real sender IP)

multi_hop_relay.compromise_resistance: An adversary must compromise all 3 relay nodes AND correlate timing to link sender and recipient. With Tor + decoy traffic this is computationally and practically infeasible.

relay_node_changes.new_capability: Relay nodes must support onion forwarding protocol — new in v1.5
relay_node_changes.forward_endpoint: POST /relay/forward — accepts onion-encrypted packet, decrypts one layer, forwards to next hop
relay_node_changes.no_logging: Forward operations never logged. Previous hop address zeroed from memory after forwarding.
relay_node_changes.version_flag: Relay nodes advertise multi-hop support in relay registry. Clients only use nodes that support it for multi-hop paths.

circuit_construction.path_diversity: No two hops in the same AS (Autonomous System) where possible
circuit_construction.path_rotation: New circuit constructed every 10 minutes or 100 messages, whichever comes first
circuit_construction.guard_nodes: First hop (guard) rotated less frequently — weekly — to reduce path discovery attacks

tor_first_architecture.description: Tor is the default connection method. Clearnet is the fallback with a visible warning indicator.

v1_vs_v15.v1_5: Default Tor. Clearnet is fallback only, shown with warning indicator in UI.

implementation.web: Connect via .onion address by default when accessed through Tor Browser. Detect Tor Browser and adjust automatically.
implementation.ios: Integrate Tor.framework (Guardian Project) — runs Tor in-process, no Orbot dependency
implementation.android: Embed Tor via NetCipher + Tor Android library — runs in-process, no Orbot dependency
implementation.fallback_indicator: Connection mode badge in chat header: green lemon slice = Tor active, yellow = clearnet fallback, red = no connection

onion_service.note: v3 onion addresses only — v2 deprecated and insecure
onion_service.self_hosted: Server operator configures .onion address in server config. Published in app and docs.
onion_service.onion_address_distribution: Hardcoded in app binary + published in SECURITY_MODEL.md + verifiable via signed announcement

no_orbot_dependency.rationale: Requiring Orbot creates a dependency the user must install separately. In-process Tor means Sublemonable works out of the box with Tor protection even if user has never heard of Orbot.
no_orbot_dependency.ios_library: Tor.framework by Guardian Project — battle tested, used by Onion Browser
no_orbot_dependency.android_library: tor-android by Guardian Project — same lineage

v1_to_v15_migration.user_impact: Existing v1 users see a one-time update prompt. All existing messages and contacts carry over. New features opt-in except Tor-first which is the new default.
v1_to_v15_migration.tor_default_rollout: Tor-first shown as new default with brief explanation on first launch after update. User can switch to clearnet if Tor is blocked in their region.
v1_to_v15_migration.second_passphrase_setup: Optional. Prompted once during update with explanation: 'Add a second passphrase to create an alternate profile.' No pressure, fully skippable.
v1_to_v15_migration.existing_accounts: Unchanged. v1.5 is fully backwards compatible with v1 server.

new: Multi-hop relay forwarding — POST /relay/forward on relay nodes
Relay capability advertisement — version flag in relay registry

phase_1.name: Storage Layer — Plausible Deniability
phase_1.why_first: Everything else builds on top of the storage layer. Get this right before building features that depend on it.
deliverables: Key slot architecture in packages/crypto — tryPassphrase(passphrase) -> VaultKey | null
Identical Argon2id timing for all passphrases — timing attack test included
Separate encrypted storage namespaces per vault on all platforms
Storage padding to normalize app storage size
Forensic protections — timestamp normalization, WAL encryption, secure delete
Second passphrase setup flow in all three apps
Decoy activity reminder system
Notification silencing when decoy vault active
Unit tests: vault isolation, timing parity, slot count non-disclosure

phase_2.name: Tor-First Network
deliverables: Embed Tor.framework in apps/ios
Embed tor-android library in apps/android
Web .onion address detection and auto-connect
Clearnet fallback with warning indicator
Connection mode badge in chat header UI
v3 onion address configuration in server
Tor bootstrap progress shown during first connect (lemon slice fills as Tor connects)

phase_3.name: Dead Drop Mode
deliverables: Dead drop server endpoints — deposit and redeem
Proof-of-work client implementation for deposit
One-time token generation in packages/crypto
QR code generation for drop token sharing
QR code scanning for drop redemption
Dead drop UI flow in all three apps
Ghost mode — all messages as dead drops
Integration tests: deposit, redeem, replay attack rejection, TTL expiry

phase_4.name: Decoy Traffic
deliverables: Background decoy envelope generator in packages/relay-client
Poisson interval distribution for timing randomization
iOS BGProcessingTask integration
Android WorkManager integration
Web Service Worker background sync
Battery impact monitoring and auto-reduce on low battery
Cover traffic intensity selector in settings UI (lemon slice fill level)
Verification: decoy envelopes indistinguishable from real in network capture

phase_5.name: Multi-Hop Relay
deliverables: Onion encryption layer in packages/crypto — 3-layer encrypt/decrypt
Circuit construction in packages/relay-client
Path selection algorithm — geographic diversity, AS diversity
Circuit rotation — 10 minutes or 100 messages
Relay node forward endpoint — POST /relay/forward
Relay capability flag in server
Multi-hop path indicator in settings UI
Integration tests: 3-hop delivery, single node compromise simulation
Performance tests: latency under multi-hop vs direct

phase_6.name: Ghost Mode Integration + Polish
deliverables: Ghost mode — ties all features together as one setting
Connection mode selector UI — Standard / Stealth / Ghost
Mode indicator in chat header — lemon slice segments show active layers
Decoy activity health check — warns if background tasks killed by OS
Full end-to-end Ghost mode test — verify no metadata leaks
Update docs/SECURITY_MODEL.md with full v1.5 onion diagram
Update CHANGELOG.md
Tag v1.5.0 release

connection_mode_badge.location: Chat header, right of contact name
connection_mode_badge.standard: Single lemon slice segment, green glow
connection_mode_badge.ghost: All segments, slow pulse, white glow
connection_mode_badge.clearnet_fallback: Yellow warning dot, tooltip: 'Tor unavailable — using direct connection'

second_passphrase_setup.label: Add a second passphrase
second_passphrase_setup.confirmation: Both passphrases confirmed, second vault initialized silently

dead_drop_ui.create: Long-press send button → 'Send as dead drop' option appears
dead_drop_ui.qr_screen: Full screen QR with lemon slice watermark, share sheet
dead_drop_ui.redeem: Main screen → compose button long-press → 'Redeem dead drop' → camera scan

cover_traffic_indicator.visual: Lemon slice with fill level showing intensity — 0 segments off, 3 segments medium, all 8 high

tor_bootstrap.first_launch: Lemon slice animates segment by segment as Tor bootstrap completes (0-100%)
tor_bootstrap.subsequent_launches: Silent — connects in background, badge updates when ready

fable_instructions.note: v1.5 blueprint — build AFTER v1 is merged and stable. Read sublemonable-MASTER.json first.
fable_instructions.start_here: Read sublemonable-MASTER.json and this file completely before writing a single line of code. v1.5 is additive — do not modify existing working features.
order: 1. Build key slot architecture and vault isolation in packages/crypto — timing parity tests mandatory
2. Add second vault setup flow and storage padding to all three apps and web
3. Add forensic protections — timestamp normalization, WAL encryption, secure delete on all platforms
4. Embed Tor.framework in iOS and tor-android in Android — in-process, no Orbot dependency
5. Implement Tor-first connection with clearnet fallback and connection mode badge
6. Add v3 onion address to server configuration
7. Build dead drop server endpoints with proof-of-work deposit
8. Build dead drop client flow — create, QR share, redeem — in all three apps
9. Build decoy traffic generator with Poisson timing in packages/relay-client
10. Integrate decoy traffic background tasks on iOS, Android, and web
11. Build onion encryption layer (3-layer) in packages/crypto
12. Build circuit construction and path selection in packages/relay-client
13. Add relay forward endpoint to server and relay nodes
14. Build Ghost mode — ties all features together with single setting
15. Build connection mode selector UI — Standard / Stealth / Ghost with lemon slice indicators
16. Full end-to-end Ghost mode integration test
17. Update SECURITY_MODEL.md with complete v1.5 onion diagram
critical_rules: PLAUSIBLE DENIABILITY IS ONLY AS STRONG AS ITS WEAKEST IMPLEMENTATION — timing parity is non-negotiable
NEVER store the number of vaults anywhere — slot count must be unknowable
NEVER label second vault as hidden, secret, or deniable anywhere in UI or code comments visible to forensics
NEVER allow notification content to reveal real vault existence when decoy is active
DEAD DROP relay endpoint requires NO authentication — proof-of-work only — account auth would defeat anonymity
DECOY envelopes must be byte-for-byte indistinguishable from real envelopes — same padding, same Tor circuit, same relay nodes
TOR is now default — clearnet is fallback — never present clearnet as the primary option
MULTI-HOP path selection must never place two hops in the same AS — defeats the purpose
GHOST MODE must be tested with network capture to verify no metadata leaks before release
ALL v1 critical rules still apply — no logging, no plaintext, parameterized SQL, JWT validation etc
ARGON2ID parameters must be identical for all vault passphrases — no optimization shortcut on decoy path
MEMORY zeroing applies doubly in v1.5 — vault keys especially must never linger in RAM
testing_requirements: Timing attack test: 1000 passphrase attempts on vault A and vault B — mean and stddev must be statistically indistinguishable
Forensic test: after using real vault, switch to decoy — no file artifacts, no timestamps, no recoverable fragments
Network capture test: 60 seconds of Ghost mode traffic — real message send must be indistinguishable from decoy traffic
Multi-hop test: simulate Relay B compromise — verify sender and recipient remain unknown to Relay B
Dead drop replay test: redeeming same token twice must return 404 on second attempt
Notification test: receive message on real vault while decoy active — no visible notification reveals real vault
